text.title: iac-patterns-composition Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for iac-patterns-composition. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Terramod: environment root modules compose from versioned reusable modules. Each environment directory has its own main.tf that calls shared modules with environment-specific inputs. State is isolated per environment. Use for any multi-environment project.
rule.rationale: Separating environment state prevents a staging apply from affecting production state; each environment's module composition can differ (e.g., staging has fewer replicas) while sharing the same module logic.

rule.statement: Landing Zone: a pre-configured environment framework providing a complete, opinionated baseline (account structure, networking, IAM, logging, monitoring) that teams deploy into without manual configuration. Use when multiple teams need standardized cloud accounts.
rule.rationale: A Landing Zone moves the org's security and compliance baseline into code, so every new account starts compliant rather than requiring manual hardening.

rule.statement: Service Catalog: a library of vetted, org-approved Terraform patterns for complex multi-resource deployments (databases, Kubernetes clusters, message queues). Teams select catalog entries rather than authoring raw Terraform. Use when teams have heterogeneous infrastructure needs.
rule.rationale: The catalog enforces org standards (encryption, tagging, IAM) at the point of infrastructure creation; teams get velocity without compromising security.

rule.statement: Hub-Spoke: a central hub account/VPC provides shared services (DNS, transit gateway, logging, security tools) to spoke workload accounts/VPCs via peering or Transit Gateway. Spokes are isolated from each other. Use in multi-account architectures.
rule.rationale: Hub-Spoke reduces blast radius (a compromised spoke cannot access other spokes), centralizes shared service costs, and simplifies network routing in large organizations.

rule.statement: State MUST be isolated per environment. NEVER share a single state file across dev, staging, and prod — a corrupted or accidental apply in dev must not affect prod state.
rule.rationale: Shared state means a terraform destroy in dev can remove resources that prod depends on via remote_state data sources.

rule.statement: Consider state isolation per component within an environment when components have different change rates. Use terraform_remote_state data sources to share outputs across state files.
rule.rationale: A monolithic environment state file means all components must be planned together; splitting by component (networking, compute, database) reduces plan time and blast radius of a failed apply.

rule.statement: State backend MUST enable encryption at rest and locking. For AWS: S3 bucket with SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS, DynamoDB table for locking. State file MUST NEVER be committed to git.
rule.rationale: State files contain resource IDs, some plaintext secrets, and the full resource graph — committing them to git is a data exposure.
